skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video. Triggers: remotion, render video from code, tsx to video, react video, programmatic video, remotion render, code to video, animated video, motion graphics code, react animation video
skill.summary: Render videos from React/Remotion TSX code to MP4 via inference.sh API
skill.author: inference-sh-7

security_audit.summary: All 43 static findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate video rendering tool using the inference.sh service. External commands are documented CLI invocations (infsh), network calls are to required external APIs, and no cryptographic or malicious patterns exist.

medium_findings.title: Shell pipe installation pattern
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation shows 'curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh' for CLI installation. This is a common installation pattern used by many legitimate tools (Docker, etc.). The skill author notes that SHA-256 checksum verification is available.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a standard CLI installation pattern with documented verification options, not malicious code execution

content.value_statement: Transform React and Remotion TSX code into MP4 videos programmatically. Perfect for developers who want to generate animations, motion graphics, and data-driven videos through code.
actual_capabilities: Render MP4 videos from React/Remotion TSX component code
Support all Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, Sequence
Configure output with custom resolution, FPS, duration, and codec
Pass dynamic props to components for data-driven videos
Stream rendering progress for long videos
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and CLI authentication
Video rendering happens on remote servers, not locally
Complex compositions may take longer to render
Limited to codec options supported by inference.sh service

use_cases.title: Generate animated explainer videos
use_cases.description: Create data-driven animated videos by passing dynamic props to React components. Perfect for automated content creation.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and developers building automated video pipelines

use_cases.title: Build motion graphics with code
use_cases.description: Design animations using React and Remotion APIs. Use spring physics, interpolation, and sequences to create professional motion graphics.

use_cases.title: Programmatic video generation
use_cases.description: Generate videos from data programmatically. Feed dynamic data into React components to create personalized video content at scale.

prompt_templates.title: Simple fade-in text
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 3-second video with fade-in text using Remotion. Use AbsoluteFill for the container, interpolate for opacity animation, white text on dark background, 1920x1080 resolution at 30fps. Render it using the remotion-render skill.

prompt_templates.title: Animated counter with props
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an animated counter from 0 to 100 that displays progress. Use the remotion-render skill with props for title and subtitle. Render at 60fps for smooth animation, 1080x1080 square format.

prompt_templates.title: Sequence animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multi-scene video with three sequential text elements. Use Remotion Sequence component to time each appearance. Render a 4-second video at 1920x1080, 30fps using remotion-render skill.

prompt_templates.title: Spring physics animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a video with a spring-animated element using Remotion spring API. Include damping and stiffness configuration. Render with custom resolution and FPS settings using the remotion-render skill.

output_examples.input: Render a video with animated counter from 0-100
output_examples.output: The skill returns a video file URL from inference.sh. Example output: { 'output': { 'video': 'https://cdn.inference.sh/...' }, 'progress': 100 }
Progress updates stream during rendering. Final output includes video URL, format details, and duration.

output_examples.input: Generate spring animation with custom props
output_examples.output: Streaming response shows progress percentage. Completed render returns video metadata and download URL.

best_practices: Start with simple compositions and increase complexity as you verify output
Use lower FPS and shorter durations during development to speed up iteration
Always specify width, height, and FPS explicitly to avoid unexpected output dimensions
Use props to make components reusable across different video outputs
anti_patterns: Do not assume local rendering - all video processing happens on inference.sh servers
Avoid extremely long videos without testing shorter versions first
Do not skip authentication setup - infsh login is required before rendering
Avoid complex nested animations without testing each component individually

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need to install the inference.sh CLI and authenticate with 'infsh login'. The skill documentation includes installation instructions.

faq.question: Where are videos rendered?
faq.answer: Videos are rendered on inference.sh remote servers. Your TSX code is sent to the service, which renders and returns the video URL.

faq.question: Can I use my own Remotion configuration?
faq.answer: The skill supports standard Remotion APIs. Complex bundler configurations may require using the inference.sh platform directly.

faq.question: How long does video rendering take?
faq.answer: Rendering time depends on video complexity, duration, and resolution. The skill supports streaming progress to monitor long renders.

faq.question: What output formats are supported?
faq.answer: The default output is MP4. You can specify codec and other parameters in the input schema.

faq.question: Can I pass dynamic data to my video?
faq.answer: Yes, use the 'props' parameter to pass dynamic values to your React component. This enables data-driven video generation.
